coredata.dc:title: A bright carbon-dot-based fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of mercury ions
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Yanhong
Zhang, Ziang
Xu, Yueling
Ma, Ming
Chen, Bo
Wei, Lin
Xiao, Lehui
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we demonstrated a convenient and green strategy for the synthesis of bright and water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) by carbonizing sodium citrate and glutathione together in a hydrothermal method for the first time. Without post surface modification, the as-synthesized CDs display fluorescence quantum yield (QY) as high as 21.03% and show superior stability not only in concentrated salt solutions but also in neutral and alkaline media. The probe exhibits selective and sensitive recognition capability towards mercury ion (Hg2+) in aqueous solution. The fluorescence of CDs can be quenched by Hg2+ through an effective electron energy transfer process. It displays a linear quenching effect toward mercury ion in the concentration range of 0–15μM with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.99. The limit of detection is determined to be 25nM at the signal to noise ratio of 3. These attractive merits would enable the extensive applications of this probe in environmental science and analytical chemistry in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Optical sensing
Mercury ion detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity analysis of the CDs for mercury ion detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum. (b) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs. (c) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs with variations of excitation wavelength. (d) TEM image of CDs.

(a) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. The peak at ~3430cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H, and the peak at 2910cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C–H. The peaks at around 1390cm−1 and 1570cm−1 are in accordance with the vibrations of C–N and N–H band, respectively. (b) XPS survey spectrum of CDs. It is evident that C is the dominant component of the CDs. Trace amount of S also exists in the nanostructure.
The effect of salt (a) and pH (b) on the fluorescence intensity of CDs.
The fluorescence quenching effect of metal ions and anions on CDs in solution with [Mn+] or [Mn−]=2mM.

(a) FL emission spectra of CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+. (b)The dependence of (F0−F)/F0 on the concentrations of Hg2+ within the range of 0–15μM. The recovery curve of (c) tap water, and (d) spring water spiked with Hg2+, respectively. The inserts are the concentration of Hg2+ vs. signal.

head.ce:title: A bright carbon-dot-based fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of mercury ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology & Traditional Chinese Medicine Research, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Phytochemical R&D of Hunan Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha, Hunan 410081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology & Traditional Chinese Medicine Research, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Phytochemical R&D of Hunan Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology & Traditional Chinese Medicine Research, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Phytochemical R&D of Hunan Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha, Hunan 410081, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we demonstrated a convenient and green strategy for the synthesis of bright and water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) by carbonizing sodium citrate and glutathione together in a hydrothermal method for the first time. Without post surface modification, the as-synthesized CDs display fluorescence quantum yield (QY) as high as 21.03% and show superior stability not only in concentrated salt solutions but also in neutral and alkaline media. The probe exhibits selective and sensitive recognition capability towards mercury ion (Hg2+) in aqueous solution. The fluorescence of CDs can be quenched by Hg2+ through an effective electron energy transfer process. It displays a linear quenching effect toward mercury ion in the concentration range of 0–15μM with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.99. The limit of detection is determined to be 25nM at the signal to noise ratio of 3. These attractive merits would enable the extensive applications of this probe in environmental science and analytical chemistry in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury ion is considered as one of the most hazardous and poisonous ions for human body and environment [1,2]. A wide range of effects in many biological processes such as mitosis impairment, DNA damage, and central nervous system damage have been attributed to mercury ions toxicity [3]. Due to its non-biodegradation feature, the contaminant can only be diluted or transformed. Therefore, it is essential to develop simple and rapid sensing approaches to precisely monitor Hg2+ in real samples. In the past decade, the commonly adopted techniques for mercury ion detection include atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS), inductive coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [4–6] and electrochemical methods [7–9]. However, these methods generally involve in complicated operation procedures, time-consuming periods, and high costs, which greatly limit their practical applications for routine assay.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention owing to their unique optical properties and promising biomedical applications [10–13]. CDs exhibit excellent fluorescent properties with chemical inertness, water solubility, low toxicity and biocompatibility [14–16]. Compared with the conventional metal quantum dots (QDs), CDs can be prepared by a simple, cost-effective and environmental friendly approach. There are various fabrication methods reported before including electrochemical oxidation, hydrothermal synthesis, laser ablation, microwave-assisted heating and so on [17–20]. Moreover, the as-synthesized CDs are usually functionalized with carboxylic/carbonyl moieties on the particle surface, where metal ions are able to coordinate readily. These advantages make them become an attractive alternative for optical sensor. For example, Wu et al. used a traditional Chinese medicine as the carbon source to synthesize CDs by hydrothermal method for Hg2+ sensing [21]. Yang et al. developed CDs from bovine serum albumin protein to detect Fe3+ ion in spring water and label living cell [22]. Although these CDs-based probes hold great promise in sensing and imaging areas, extensive research is still necessary to explore the full potential of these materials for the application of smart sensors.

The commonly adopted methods for CDs preparation are usually involving in hazardous chemical materials and complicated experimental procedures while the resulting CDs still exhibited very low fluorescence QY. In this work, we proposed a convenient and green strategy for the synthesis of highly luminescent and water-soluble CDs by carbonizing sodium citrate and glutathione in a hydrothermal method for the first time. The measured QY of the CDs is as high as 21.03% with quinine sulfate as a reference. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements illustrate that the surface of CDs is coated with abundant of functional groups (e.g. –OH), providing an versatile route for further chemical and biological modification. These CDs display superior colloidal stability not only in concentrated salt solutions but also in neutral and alkaline solutions. Interestingly, these CDs exhibit a specific and dosage dependent fluorescence quenching effect toward mercury ion in aqueous solution. With this sensor, excellent linear relationship between the quenching degree of the CDs and the concentration of Hg2+ was established in the range of 0–15μM (i.e., 0–3mg/L). Limit-of-detection as low as 25nM (i.e., 5μg/L) (3σ) was achieved, which is lower than results reported before [23–27]. As a consequence, these CDs would find promising applications for the detection of Hg2+ in real sample in the further.

Sodium citrate, glutathione (oxidization), and quinine sulfate (98%, suitable for fluorescence) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (China). All metal ion stock solutions were prepared from nitrate salts or chlorate salts. All other reagents (purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China) were analytical grade and used without further purification. The UV–vis absorption spectrum measurement was performed on a Specord S600 spectrometer (Analytik Jena, Germany). Fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Hatachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Ltd., Japan). The FT-IR spectra were measured on Thermo Nicolet 6700 Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. U.S.A.). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated by using K-Alpha 1063 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, U.S.A.) with a mono X-ray source Al Ka excitation. Binding energy calibration was based on C1s at 284.6eV. A pH meter (PHS-3W, Inesa, China) was utilized to measure the pH values of aqueous solutions.

To prepare fluorescent CDs, typically, sodium citrate (0.1g) and glutathione (0.01g) were dissolved in deionized water (3mL). Then the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (30mL) and heated at 200°C for 4h. After the reaction, the mixture was cooled down to room temperature naturally. Then the yellow aqueous solution was purified by filtration through 0.1µm filter to remove large particles and stored at 4°C before use.

The relative fluorescence QY of the CDs was measured by using quinine sulfate (QY 54%) as a reference. The quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4. By using the equation of Ø=Ø R ×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
n
2
n R
2
, where Ø is the measured QY of CDs, Ø R is the QY of quinine sulfate, I is integrated fluorescence intensity, n and A are the refractive index and optical density of the solution. Based on this equation, the relative fluorescence QY of CDs can then be readily determined.

The CDs were prepared through hydrothermal treatment of sodium citrate in the presence of glutathione at 200°C for 4h. To explore the optical properties of the CDs, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CDs in aqueous solution were investigated as shown in Fig. 1a and b. The CDs display a maximum absorption peak at 355nm and a blue emission peak at 450nm. When changing the excitation wavelength, the emission spectrum didn't shift simultaneously in comparison with the previously reported CDs which typically changed as a function of the excitation wavelength [28], Fig. 1c. This indicates a good purity of the CDs. The TEM image showed that the size of as-synthesized CDs was about 2.5nm, Fig. 1d. By using quinine sulfate as a reference, the fluorescence QY of CDs was measured to be 21.03%. To the best of our knowledge, this result is better than the previous work that also obtained by citric acid derivatives [29].

Since Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy is a robust method to characterize the functional group of a chemical structure, the surface functionality of CDs was then explored with FT-IR as shown in Fig. 2a. Amount of functional groups were observed from the FT-IR spectra. For example, the peak at ~3430cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H, and the peak at 2910cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C–H. The peaks at around 1390cm−1 and 1570cm−1 are in accordance with the vibrations of C–N and N–H band, respectively. As a consequence, it can conclude that the surface of this water-soluble CDs is occupied with hydrophilic groups (e. g. –OH) and N-related groups (e. g. –NH).

To gain further insight into the surface functionality and elemental states of the as-prepared CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted. As shown in Fig. 2b, four peaks at around 284.8, 400.1, 531.0 and 163.9eV are presented in the XPS survey spectrum and correspond to C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p core levels, respectively. These results revealed that the CDs were mainly composed of 50.19% (C), 45.81% (O) 2.59% (N) and 1.42% (S).

From the consideration of their potential sensing or imaging application capability, it is significant to explore the stability of these CDs not only from the aspect of colloidal stability but also from the performance of the optical property resistant to chemical stimuli. As displayed in Fig. 3a, the fluorescence intensity of CDs in KCl solutions with different concentrations was measured. Even in KCl solution with concentration higher than 2M, the fluorescence intensity of CDs is still unchanged, indicative of its potential applications as an optical probe.

To realize fluorescence-based sensing, pH is one of the most important influence factors. On this account, the CDs were treated in different pH solutions for 2h and then compared the fluorescence intensity with that in DI water. As shown in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs is insensitive to the pH change in a range of 7–14. These results indicate that this probe can be potentially used in neutral media such as live cell system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity analysis of the CDs for mercury ion detection
ce:section.ce:para:
According to the FT-IR and XPS characterizations, the stability and hydrophilicity of the CDs in aqueous system are improved due to many functional groups on the surface. Therefore, these CDs might exhibit high affinity toward metal ions through chelation. To explore the response of CDs toward metal ions, the fluorescence intensities of CDs were then analyzed in the presence of various metal ions including K+, Na+, Mg2+, La3+, Al3+, Co2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Hg2+ Ba2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, and Li+ under the same concentration (2mM), respectively. As shown in Fig. 4, no obvious fluorescence decrease was observed after the addition of metal ions, except Hg2+, where a significant fluorescence quenching effect was noted. Meanwhile, the influence from anions is also negligible. This fluorescence quenching effect might be due to the non-radiative electron transfer from the excited state to the d-orbital of Hg2+ [30]. It is worth to point out that, the addition of Hg2+ decreased the fluorescence intensity of the CDs, but had no effect on the shape of fluorescence spectrum, Fig. 5a. The fluorescence quenching effect can be described by the following the normalized fluorescence quenching efficiency:
(
F
0
- F
)
/ F
0
= K
sv
[
Q
]

Here, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the CDs at 450nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+ ions, respectively. Ksv is the fluorescence quenching constant, Q is the concentration of the analyte. The concentration dependent fluorescence quenching plot is shown in Fig. 5b. It displays a linear quenching effect toward mercury ion in the concentration range of 0–15μM (i.e., 0–3mg/L) with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.99. The limit of detection is determined to be 25nM (i.e., 5μg/L) at the signal to noise ratio of 3.

In order to evaluate the performance of this probe in real sample analysis, we used two water samples from the tap water, spring water (Yuelu Mountain, Hunan Province, China) and spiked them with Hg2+ at different concentrations respectively. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs decreased gradually when the concentration of Hg2+ was increased. The plot of the measured Hg2+ as a function of the content spiked is shown in Fig. 5c and d. Although many trace metal ions might exist in the real sample, the good linear relationship of these recovery curves indicates that the CDs can specifically and accurately distinguish Hg2+ in the two samples. These results demonstrate that this sensing system would find promising application for other real sample analysis in the future such as development of test paper for waste water assay.

In summary, we demonstrated a simple and green strategy for the hydrothermal synthesis of water soluble and bright CDs. The as-synthesized CDs exhibit narrow size distribution, excellent fluorescence property, and good colloidal stability in different surroundings such as concentrated salt solutions and alkaline medium. The abundance of functional groups on the CDs surface not only play a central role for the effective stabilization of CDs in different media but also afford a versatile platform for the specific recognition of metal ions, i.e. Hg2+. In contrast to the previously reported strategies for CDs synthesis, the method proposed herein does not require further chemical modification, the sensor displays good reproducibility and the novel strategy eliminated the necessity for organic solvent, which make the process more environmental friendly. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of CDs can be effectively quenched by Hg2+ within several minutes by one-step rapid operation. We believe that the CDs prepared by this strategy would display promising applications in various areas, including environmental science, analytical chemistry and so on.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by NSFC (21405045, 21522502), Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (China, NCET-13-0789), and Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province for Distinguished Young Scholar (14JJ1017).
